reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: No mandatory style violations — all CodeBlock examples use the correct <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> form, noun-form H2s match the example, frontmatter shape matches exactly.

Heading structure and H2 order precisely follow the example page (When to use → When NOT to use → Basic example → resource-specific sections → Connecting to other resources → Referenceable parameters → API Reference → FAQ).
The 'When NOT to use' section is concrete and opinionated with four clearly distinct anti-patterns, each linked to the right alternative resource.

Event archiving section includes decision support: when to enable, a retention-days value with rationale, a Tip callout with an explicit recommendation, and a cost-context sentence — not just configuration.
Partner event sources section leads with 'Most teams do not need this', which is exactly the opinionated framing the audience needs for an advanced feature.

'Subscribing to events' names all three subscriber types (Lambda, batch jobs, SQS queues) upfront, then deep-dives on Lambda with a full, copyable working config + handler code. The delivery-failure H3 is a useful addition.
Connecting to other resources matches the example's env-var table format exactly and provides a full config + application code walkthrough.
The Warning callout about fan-out semantics (all matching rules fire independently) is precisely placed where the reader would make the mistake.

FAQ has exactly 10 questions spanning all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (archiving replay, default bus, pattern filtering, container services), AWS-specific (pricing model, max event size, retry/failure behavior), and comparison (vs SQS, vs SNS, vs Kinesis).
Application code examples (EventBridgeClient PutEvents, Lambda handler receiving event.detail) are realistic and directly copyable.
problems: The 'Subscribing to events' section enumerates three target types (Lambda, batch jobs, SQS queues) but only provides a working code example for Lambda. The documentation-quality discipline requires per-variant depth. An SQS-queue-as-subscriber example is particularly valuable here because the event-bus → SQS fan-out pattern is explicitly recommended multiple times in the page's own FAQ and Warning callout, yet a reader looking to implement it has no config example to copy.

The opening paragraph mentions 'You pay per event with no capacity to manage' but no pricing context line appears — the SQS example page has a pricing paragraph immediately after the opening paragraph. EventBridge does have a well-known pricing model (pay-per-event on custom buses) even though no source file provides exact figures. Omitting the paragraph is correct per the rules (no sourced pricing data), but the asymmetry may confuse readers who rely on the page rhythm from other messaging resource pages.

The 'Delivery failures' H3 under 'Subscribing to events' briefly mentions inputTransformer/input/inputPath but does not give the reader enough to decide whether they need these features — a one-sentence description of what each controls would satisfy decision-support requirements without adding length.
optionalImprovements: Add a second code example in 'Subscribing to events' showing an SQS queue subscribing to the event bus (type: 'event-bus' on the SqsQueue's events property). Even a compact 10-line example would complete the per-variant depth requirement and give readers the fan-out pattern they're already being pointed toward.

In the 'Delivery failures' H3, add one sentence clarifying what input/inputPath/inputTransformer each do (e.g. 'input replaces the whole event payload, inputPath extracts a sub-field, inputTransformer lets you construct a new payload from extracted values') so readers can decide whether they need payload shaping without following the cross-link.

The EventBridge pricing model (pay per million events published to custom buses, archive storage charged separately) is well-known and does not require a source file to justify. Consider adding a short pricing note matching the SQS example's rhythm — phrased carefully as an AWS fact, not a Stacktape claim.

The 'Subscribing to events' section says 'See the event bus trigger reference for the full pattern syntax' but does not mention that the eventPattern supports nested detail matching, prefix, numeric ranges, and existence checks until the FAQ. Pulling that sentence into the prose of the section (before the example) would give readers enough confidence to attempt pattern-writing without leaving the page.

FAQ question 9 ('Can container services subscribe to event bus events?') answers with a workaround but does not explain *why* containers cannot subscribe directly (they lack an events trigger system; their model is always polling). Adding one sentence on the root cause makes the answer more satisfying and more likely to be cited as a standalone snippet.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Strictly follows the example page's structure — noun-form H2s, same relative ordering of shared sections (When to use, When NOT to use, Basic example, Connecting to other resources, Referenceable parameters, API Reference, FAQ), matching frontmatter keys, and the same MDX component palette (<Tip>, <Warning>, <CodeBlock>, <ReferenceableParams>, <ApiReference>).
connectTo environment variable claim is correctly grounded: the JSDoc says EventBus injects ARN, and the draft shows only STP_ORDER_BUS_ARN with no unsupported additions.

The <Warning> about fan-out vs competing-consumer semantics (all matching rules fire independently, unlike SQS) is accurate, useful, and positioned at exactly the right moment in the Subscribing section.
When NOT to use is concrete and actionable — each bullet names a real alternative (SQS, SNS, Kinesis, HTTP API Gateway) instead of generic language.

FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (env var, archiving replay, default bus), AWS use-case (pricing, max event size, retry behavior), and comparison (event bus vs SQS, vs SNS, vs Kinesis).
Correctly omits the Alarms section — EventBusProps has no alarms property and StpAlarmEnabledResource in alarms.d.ts does not include EventBus.
Both handler and publisher code samples are realistic and copyable, showing the full round-trip from consumer to producer.
Partner event sources section correctly frames the feature as niche ('most teams do not need this') with a clear signal for who should use it.
problems: The archiveArn referenceable parameter (present in source as EventBusReferencableParam = 'arn' | 'archiveArn') receives no prose mention anywhere on the page. Readers needing to reference the archive ARN from another resource have no guidance that it exists.

The Delivery failures subsection states 'EventBridge retries failed deliveries for up to 24 hours with exponential backoff' as a bare fact in the main body without attributing it as AWS EventBridge behavior. Per style guidance, AWS-side behaviors should be framed as 'AWS EventBridge...' or 'Underneath, this uses AWS EventBridge, which...' to separate Stacktape from AWS layers.

The opening paragraph is approximately 50 words — within the 40–75 word range, but noticeably leaner than the example's denser opener. The example page follows its opening with a dedicated pricing-context sentence. The draft folds the pricing signal ('You pay per event') into the opener and omits the standalone line. Since no pricing data exists in the source files this is acceptable, but a brief standalone pricing sentence ('EventBridge uses pay-per-event pricing with no minimum fee or idle charges') would match the example's rhythm.
optionalImprovements: In the Event archiving section, add one sentence noting that the archive ARN (available as a referenceable parameter) can be used with $ResourceParam() to reference the archive from another resource — for example, an automated replay Lambda. This surfaces archiveArn without inventing connectTo behavior.

Prefix the 24-hour retry claim with 'AWS EventBridge retries...' rather than presenting it as a neutral fact. This keeps the AWS/Stacktape boundary clean and matches the style guidance on attributing AWS-side behavior.

Add a short standalone pricing context sentence immediately after the opening paragraph — e.g., 'Amazon EventBridge uses pay-per-event pricing with no minimum fee or upfront capacity commitment.' This aligns the opening rhythm with the example page, even without sourced dollar figures.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Self-contained opening paragraph answers 'what is this and why does it matter' in about 50 words — extractable verbatim by AI engines.
Every opt-in feature section (Event archiving, Partner event sources) includes concrete when-to-enable guidance, when-default-suffices signal, cost/tradeoff, and an opinionated recommendation.
Code examples are complete and copy-pasteable; each major feature has its own working Stacktape config example plus application-level handler code (EventBridge PutEvents, Lambda handler).
connectTo env var table uses the concrete example resource name ('orderBus' → 'STP_ORDER_BUS_ARN') matching the source JSDoc, not a generic placeholder.
Warning callout correctly flags the key EventBridge fan-out behavior (independent rule evaluation, no competing-consumer semantics), which is a common source of bugs.
Tip callout delivers an opinionated production recommendation ('Enable archiving for production event buses') rather than just documenting the API.
FAQ has exactly 10 questions spanning all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, underlying AWS service questions, and choosing/comparison questions.
Heading style (noun-form H2s), MDX component palette (Tip + Warning + CodeBlock + ReferenceableParams + ApiReference), and ApiReference placement all match the example page exactly.
'Subscribing to events' section explicitly names all three subscriber types (Lambda, batch jobs, SQS queues) before diving into the primary use case, avoiding the implied-only-option problem.
Omission of an Alarms section is correct and source-grounded: EventBus is absent from StpAlarmEnabledResource in the source, unlike SQS queues.
problems: 'Subscribing to events' section names three subscriber types (Lambda functions, batch jobs, SQS queues) but provides a detailed code example only for Lambda. Batch jobs and SQS queues are one-liner descriptions. While the per-variant depth rule applies primarily to union-typed properties on the resource itself, readers routing to batch jobs or SQS queues get no concrete guidance here and must navigate to those pages.

No pricing context line after the opening paragraph. The example SQS queue page includes a pricing paragraph immediately after the opening. EventBridge pricing data is absent from the provided source files, so omission is technically correct, but creates a structural gap compared to the example. Readers who come to this page specifically to assess cost have no landing point for that question until the FAQ.

The 'archiveArn' referenceable parameter (from EventBusReferencableParam = 'arn' | 'archiveArn') is surfaced by the ReferenceableParams component but never mentioned in prose. Readers who need the archive ARN (for cross-account replay or monitoring) have no signal that it exists unless they scroll to the referenceable parameters section.
optionalImprovements: In 'Subscribing to events', add a two-to-three sentence treatment for each of the two non-Lambda subscriber types — a batch job example showing how an event starts an async job, and an SQS queue pattern that shows routing events to a queue for container polling. These are short additions and fulfill per-variant depth for the section.

Add a one-line pricing context sentence after the opening paragraph (e.g. 'Amazon EventBridge charges per million custom events published to event buses; there is no charge for events delivered to targets or matched by rules.') — this is well-established AWS pricing behavior and the FAQ already covers it, so it can be mentioned up front without violating the 'never invent pricing not in source' rule since it describes the pricing model, not a dollar figure.

In the 'Referenceable parameters' section intro or in 'Event archiving', add one sentence noting that the archive ARN is also exposed as a referenceable parameter (archiveArn), accessible via $ResourceParam(), for use cases such as triggering archive replays from external tooling.

FAQ Q8 describes EventBridge's 24-hour retry behavior — consider adding a sentence distinguishing between delivery failures (which get retried) and rule evaluation failures (which do not), as this is a common confusion point.

issues.statement: The 'Subscribing to events' section shows only a Lambda function code example. Batch jobs and SQS queues — the other two declared subscriber types — get one-liner mentions with no code examples or per-variant guidance.
issues.reason: Per the 'Per-variant equal depth' documentation-quality rule, when a section names multiple variants, each should get roughly equivalent treatment including a working code example and when-to-use context. The draft names three subscriber types but only Lambda gets a code example and detailed treatment. Batch jobs and SQS queues get zero examples and no guidance on when to prefer one subscriber type over another.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a brief code example for at least the SQS queue subscriber variant (since it has unique properties like `messageGroupId` for FIFO queues), or add a comparison table showing when to use Lambda vs SQS vs Batch as event bus targets. Alternatively, add a sentence explaining that the event bus trigger configuration is identical across all three types (linking to the trigger reference page) and note the SQS-specific `messageGroupId` option.

evidence.quote: interface SqsQueueEventBusIntegrationProps extends EventBusIntegrationProps { messageGroupId?: string; }

issues.statement: The FAQ answer about container services says 'route events from the bus to an SQS queue using an event bus trigger on the queue, then have the container poll the queue using the AWS SDK with connectTo'.
issues.reason: The recommended workaround says the container should 'poll the queue using the AWS SDK'. This is misleading — polling SQS manually with the AWS SDK is an uncommon and error-prone pattern. Container services in Stacktape can use SQS as a trigger (via SqsIntegration in their events config), which is the standard and simpler approach. The FAQ should recommend the trigger-based pattern rather than manual SDK polling.
issues.suggestedFix: Rewrite the workaround to: 'route events from the bus to an SQS queue using an event bus trigger on the queue, then configure an SQS trigger on the container to consume messages.' Or mention that the container can use an SQS event source mapping if supported, or at minimum note that manual polling is one option while an SQS trigger (on a Lambda function) is simpler.

issues.statement: The 'Event archiving' section recommends enabling archiving for production event buses but does not mention the archiveArn referenceable parameter.
issues.reason: The EventBusReferencableParam type includes both 'arn' and 'archiveArn'. The draft documents archiving behavior well but never mentions that the archive ARN is available as a referenceable parameter via $ResourceParam(). Users who enable archiving and want to reference the archive (e.g., for replay automation) would benefit from knowing this parameter exists. The ReferenceableParams component will show it, but a brief prose mention in the archiving section would help discoverability.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a one-sentence note in the archiving section or after the ReferenceableParams component mentioning that the archive ARN is available via $ResourceParam('myBus', 'archiveArn') when archiving is enabled.

positiveFindings: All three subscriber types (Lambda, Batch, SQS) are confirmed by source type definitions — each resource has EventBusIntegration in its events union type, and SQS has a dedicated resolver implementation.
The connectTo environment variable claim (STP_ORDER_BUS_ARN) correctly matches the ResourceAccessProps JSDoc which documents EventBus → 'ARN' as the injected parameter.
The events:PutEvents IAM action claim is confirmed by role-helpers.ts in the resolver code.
All EventBusProps properties (eventSourceName, archivation.enabled with @default false, archivation.retentionDays) are accurately documented and match the source type definitions.

The EventBusIntegrationProps properties (eventBusName, eventBusArn, useDefaultBus, eventPattern, onDeliveryFailure, input, inputPath, inputTransformer) are all accurately described and match the source.
All cross-links point to valid routes in the documentation navigation index.
The ApiReference uses the correct definitionName 'EventBusProps' matching the source type.
'When NOT to use' is correctly structured as an H2 peer section, not nested under 'When to use'.
The FAQ has exactly 10 Q&A pairs spanning Stacktape-specific, AWS service, and comparison categories.
Code examples use valid Stacktape class names (EventBus, LambdaFunction, StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging) and proper defineConfig pattern with focus markers hiding imports.
verifierResults.summary: The draft is factually accurate and well-grounded in the source. All Stacktape-specific claims about EventBusProps, connectTo behavior, referenceable parameters, and subscriber types are confirmed by the source files. The three subscriber types (Lambda, Batch, SQS) are all verified — SQS queues have a dedicated SqsQueueEventBusIntegration type and resolver. The only substantive issues are documentation-quality concerns: the subscriber section shows unequal depth across variants (only Lambda gets a code example), the FAQ workaround for container services recommends manual SDK polling instead of the standard SQS trigger pattern, and the archiveArn referenceable parameter goes unmentioned in prose. No high-severity factual issues found.

The draft is mostly grounded in the provided EventBus, EventBusIntegration, and connectTo type sources. The main issues are a few Stacktape-specific or overly exact claims that go beyond the supplied files, plus several general EventBridge operational claims stated with more certainty than the provided sources support.

issues.statement: “Use `connectTo` ... Stacktape grants `events:PutEvents` IAM permission and injects the bus ARN as an environment variable.”
issues.reason: The provided source supports that `connectTo` gives EventBus publish access and injects `ARN`, but it does not name the exact IAM action `events:PutEvents`. That action is plausible AWS behavior, but the exact policy action is not grounded in the supplied Stacktape source.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the sentence to: “Use `connectTo` ... Stacktape grants the workload permission to publish events to the bus and injects the bus ARN as `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_ARN`.”

evidence.quote:
List the names of resources this workload needs to communicate with. Stacktape automatically:
   * - **Grants IAM permissions** (e.g., S3 read/write, SQS send/receive)
   * - **Opens network access** (security group rules for databases, Redis)
   * - **Injects environment variables** with connection details: `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]`

issues.statement: “SQS provides built-in retry, dead-letter queues, and visibility timeouts that EventBridge does not.”
issues.reason: The provided Stacktape source shows EventBus integrations support an SQS destination for delivery failures. The blanket claim that EventBridge does not provide dead-letter behavior is too broad and conflicts with the documented `onDeliveryFailure` property. Visibility timeouts remain SQS-specific, but the sentence groups several features together incorrectly.
issues.suggestedFix: Rewrite as: “SQS is better for point-to-point work queues because it gives consumers queue semantics such as visibility timeouts and message polling. Event bus triggers can route failed deliveries to SQS with `onDeliveryFailure`, but EventBridge is primarily an event router, not a task queue.”

issues.statement: “Disabling archiving deletes the archive and all stored events permanently.”
issues.reason: The source supports “Disabling deletes the archive,” but it does not explicitly state permanence or “all stored events.” That may be true AWS behavior, but the supplied Stacktape source only grounds archive deletion.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the source wording more narrowly: “Disabling archiving deletes the archive.” If you want to mention loss of stored events, phrase it cautiously as an AWS archive implication, not a Stacktape-specific guarantee.

evidence.quote: #### Enable event archiving. Disabling deletes the archive.

issues.statement: “EventBridge event archiving stores a copy of every event published to the bus...”
issues.reason: The Stacktape source says archiving stores events so they can be replayed later, but it does not specify that every event published to the bus is archived. “Every event” is a stronger claim than the source supports.
issues.suggestedFix: Rewrite to: “EventBridge event archiving stores events published to the bus so you can replay them later.”

evidence.quote: #### Archive events to store and replay them later. Useful for debugging, testing, or error recovery.

issues.statement: “The event bus trigger supports `onDeliveryFailure` to route events that fail delivery to an SQS queue.”
issues.reason: The source says `onDeliveryFailure` is for events that fail to be delivered, but adds the caveat “In rare cases.” The draft omits that caveat and presents delivery failure routing as a general failure-handling path, which could overstate the scope of what EventBridge sends to this destination.
issues.suggestedFix: Add the source caveat: “In rare delivery-failure cases, `onDeliveryFailure` can route failed events to an SQS queue.”

evidence.quote:
#### A destination for events that fail to be delivered to the target.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * In rare cases, an event might fail to be delivered. This property specifies an SQS queue where failed events will be sent.

issues.statement: “You pay per event with no capacity to manage.” and “Pay-per-event pricing.”
issues.reason: No pricing source file or price quote is provided for EventBus. The pricing model is a general AWS fact, but the prompt’s pricing rules say to include concrete pricing context near the top only when pricing data is present in provided source files. Here it is not.
issues.suggestedFix: Either remove the pricing sentence near the top or phrase it as general AWS context in the FAQ without implying source-backed Stacktape pricing data. Do not add concrete prices unless a pricing source is provided.

issues.statement: “Custom event buses charge per million events published. Events matched by rules and delivered to targets are included at no additional charge. Archive and replay incur separate storage and replay costs.”
issues.reason: These are AWS pricing details, but no pricing files are included in the supplied source list. The general pricing model is allowed in FAQ, yet the detailed claim about matched and delivered events being included at no additional charge may drift and is not grounded here.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the FAQ answer higher level: “EventBridge generally charges based on event volume, with separate charges for archive and replay. Check current AWS EventBridge pricing for exact units and regional rates.”

issues.statement: “EventBridge retries failed deliveries for up to 24 hours with exponential backoff.”
issues.reason: This is a general AWS behavior claim and may be acceptable, but it is not present in the provided Stacktape source. Because the source only documents Stacktape’s `onDeliveryFailure` destination, the draft should avoid making the retry duration sound like Stacktape-documented behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Phrase as AWS context: “AWS EventBridge retries failed target invocations according to EventBridge retry behavior; configure `onDeliveryFailure` to send undeliverable events to SQS.”

positiveFindings: The draft uses class-based `defineConfig` examples inside `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with `label: 'TypeScript'` and does not hand-author YAML Stacktape configuration.
The API reference uses `<ApiReference definitionName="EventBusProps" />`, which matches the source type name.
The `ReferenceableParams` component is appropriate because the source exposes `EventBusReferencableParam = 'arn' | 'archiveArn'`.
The `connectTo` environment variable example `STP_ORDER_BUS_ARN` correctly follows the documented `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]` naming pattern for a resource named `orderBus`.
The draft correctly documents the core EventBus properties visible in source: `eventSourceName`, `archivation.enabled`, and optional `archivation.retentionDays`.

verifierResults.summary: API completeness audit found no publication-blocking gaps for the Event Bus page. The draft documents the two primary EventBusProps properties, acknowledges the important trigger-side options it introduces, and the Stacktape config examples demonstrate the sections they appear in.
positiveFindings: The primary EventBusProps surface is covered: eventSourceName and archivation are both documented with focused examples.
The archivation example demonstrates the documented property path archivation.enabled and retentionDays instead of using a generic scaffold.
The event bus trigger section names the three bus-selection options from EventBusIntegrationProps: eventBusName, eventBusArn, and useDefaultBus.
The connectTo section uses the canonical resource-name-dependent environment variable pattern with STP_ORDER_BUS_ARN for a resource named orderBus.
The page includes exactly one ApiReference component with definitionName="EventBusProps".
